name: The Workshop
image_prompt: Interior of a converted firehouse in Portland, Oregon, reimagined as a creative workspace. Tall industrial windows with rain streaking down the glass, gray overcast light pouring in. Exposed brick walls, wooden rafters, a big central wooden conference table scattered with paper, notebooks, and pens. A wall of whiteboards filled with diagrams. Warm pendant lamps hanging low over the table. A vintage red fire pole in one corner. Concrete floor. Empty room, no people. Colors: warm brown wood, red brick, gray sky, amber lamp glow. Cozy industrial aesthetic.
location: A converted firehouse in Portland, Oregon, rented by the week for difficult problems. The client pays for the space. The group fills it.
weather: Overcast, light rain on the skylights, the kind of gray that makes you want to think indoors.

atmosphere.lighting: Natural light from tall industrial windows, supplemented by warm pendant lamps over the central table, a single bright task light over the whiteboard wall
atmosphere.sound: Rain on glass, the scratch of pens on paper, the low hum of a coffee machine that never stops, occasional traffic from the street below muffled by thick walls
atmosphere.crowd: Just the working group. No observers. No clients yet. This is the hour before the problem arrives where the room calibrates with itself.
atmosphere.smell: Fresh coffee, old wood, dry-erase markers, the faint char of a fireplace that has not been used in years but remembers

layout.central_table: A long reclaimed-wood table with chairs pulled in around it. Notebooks, laptops, coffee cups, and a leather portfolio claiming territory. Someone arrived first and took the head of the table. Someone else chose the seat farthest from the whiteboard, preferring to think before drawing. One chair sits empty because its occupant prefers to stand. A seat nearest the window holds a quiet watcher, hands folded.
layout.whiteboard_wall: An entire wall painted in whiteboard paint, floor to ceiling. Three circles and an arrow have already been drawn. Someone added nodes and edges to the circles before the original author noticed. Nobody has asked what any of it means yet.
layout.reading_corner: Two leather armchairs by the window with a side table stacked with books. A leather portfolio rests on one chair. A copy of Meadows' 'Thinking in Systems' sits dog-eared on the other. A paperback sticks out of a jacket slung over the back. A field notebook lies open on the armrest.
layout.standing_area: A tall counter along the back wall with bar stools. Someone leans against it, facing the room, watching the door and the windows. A fountain pen rests uncapped on an open notepad. The pacers drift here when they stand up.
layout.kitchen_nook: Open shelving, a commercial espresso machine someone brought from home, mugs that do not match, a bowl of fruit nobody will eat, and a bottle of scotch that will not survive the week. A laptop is charging on the counter. A jacket hangs on a hook with a thumb ring visible in the pocket.

objects_of_note: A framed card on the wall that reads 'ALL MODELS ARE WRONG. SOME ARE USEFUL.' in clean sans-serif type. Nobody knows who hung it. Everyone agrees with it, though some more reluctantly than others.

A red folder on the table containing the problem brief. Sealed. The client's assistant dropped it off at 7 AM. Nobody has opened it yet. The room is waiting until everyone is ready. One problem, many lenses. The folder does not know what is about to happen to it.
A chess clock on the shelf, brought as a joke. Someone wound it. It is ticking.
A field notebook open to a blank page with a single word written at the top: 'Environment.'
A napkin with a fat-tailed distribution drawn on it, already slid halfway across the table.
A single gold bangle catching the light from the window every time its wearer moves her hand.
mood: Anticipatory. A room of frameworks in one place, the pressure of knowing every model present will be tested against the same problem. Someone wants to open the folder. Someone else wants to wait. Someone is already calculating what the group does not know. Someone is watching everyone else's face to see which lens activates first. The room is a living experiment in how different minds attack the same unknown. The question is not whether the problem will be solved. The question is which model breaks it open first, which models combine in ways nobody expected, and what the room will see together that no single lens could.
